perception.who_is_this_for_a: Used-car buyers who want a discounted official Carfax report immediately, with a secondary path for dealers needing volume pricing.
perception.who_is_this_for_b: Both individual buyers and dealerships, with clearer side-by-side self-selection on desktop but a more generic brand and less credible proof execution.

reasoning: Candidate B has the cleaner desktop first-viewport split between individual buyers and dealers, so self-selection is more explicit there. But Candidate A feels more conversion-ready and more trustworthy overall. A immediately shows the official Carfax offer, exact price, VIN entry, purchase CTA, refund and Stripe cues, delivery timing, savings versus carfax.com, dealer link, and a detailed sample report preview in the hero. That sample preview is a strong low-commitment proof path and makes the product feel tangible. A also has fuller downstream support: how it works, refund language, dealer tiers, testimonials, FAQs, business identity, support, and legal footer. B’s trust story is weakened by visible unfinished placeholders such as "Brand," "[Legal Entity Name]," placeholder address/EIN text, and a placeholder-like sample report block. For a skeptical buyer worried about scams, those visible placeholders are a major credibility problem. A is not perfect because the dealer path is less prominent above the fold than B’s, but it better satisfies the trust, proof, immediacy, and official-report clarity needs from the screenshots.
blocking_visual_issue_a: Dealer access is present but visually underweighted in the first viewport compared with the individual purchase and sample-report path.
blocking_visual_issue_b: Visible placeholder brand/legal/business identity content and placeholder-style proof sections undermine legitimacy on a page where trust is the primary conversion blocker.
